A top lawmaker from Australia’s far-right One Nation party has grabbed headlines after exploding at a misbehaving dog as he was grilled on live TV.

“Sorry, it’s the dog,” he told the journalist.

articles.title: Trump imposes 50% tariffs on Canadian goods, citing disputes over cars, alcohol and cheese
articles.summary:
US President Donald Trump on Monday imposed 50 per cent tariffs on most Canadian goods, declaring that Canada has unfairly discriminated against American cars, alcohol and dairy products.
The move could unleash a new wave of economic chaos, with risks of higher inflation and further fraying of relations between two nations that had been closely woven together before Trump’s return to the White House.
The administration official previewing the action said that Canada was one of the only nations.....

articles.title: Trump order targets China-linked military mineral supply chains
articles.summary:
US President Donald Trump on Monday signed an executive order aimed at reducing dependence on China for defence-related critical minerals, directing military contractors to shift away from Beijing-linked supply chains beginning in January 2027.
Without naming China, Trump’s executive order stresses the need to maintain US military dominance “in an era of renewed great power competition”.
“The United States must secure its supply chains against physical, cyber, and economic subversion,” it...

articles.title: ‘Systemic leap’: China’s drug innovation push yields bumper crop
articles.summary:
Domestically produced novel drugs accounted for more than 80 per cent of market approvals in China in the first half of the year, reflecting a “leap” in the quality of the country’s pharmaceutical innovation, according to a government spokesman.
A total of 38 innovative drugs were cleared for sale in the six-month period, of which 31 were home-grown medicines, said Ministry of Industry and Information Technology spokesman Tao Qing, at a media briefing in Beijing on Monday.
The approvals, along.....

articles.title: Chinese stocks rose by 0.4 per cent as state-backed insurers tout long-term investment
articles.summary:
Chinese stocks rose, extending a rebound spurred by regulatory support after global-sell-offs dampened sentiment.
The CSI 300 Index climbed 0.4 per cent as of 9.35am local time and the tech-heavy Star Market 50 index added 0.4 per cent. Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index rose 0.3 per cent.
In the latest sign of government support for stocks, five state-backed insurers including Ping An Insurance Group and the People’s Insurance Group of China, all touted the long-term investment values of Chinese...

articles.title: Sri Lanka deploys military drones to fight worst dengue outbreak in almost a decade
articles.summary:
Sri Lanka’s ⁠public health services and hospitals are battling the worst outbreak of mosquito-borne dengue in nearly a decade, and turning to the island nation’s armed forces to help curb the spread of the disease.
The efforts range from air force drones tracking stagnating rooftop pools of water after monsoon rains to military and police officers helping to inspect homes, building sites and schools in a campaign to stamp out mosquito-breeding sites.
With 53 deaths and 76,044 infections since it...

articles.title: To prosper, China must become a magnet for international students
articles.summary:
Last month, 12.9 million students in China sat for the National Higher Education Entrance Examination, or gaokao, the number sliding for a second year, and down from 13.3 million last year. Given that births have fallen below 8 million annually to a record low, in 18 years, the country’s more than 3,100 universities will find themselves fighting over an even smaller cohort.
This will coincide with a growing global competition for talent. In a knowledge-driven economy, the countries that prosper....

articles.title: Squally thunderstorms set to hit parts of Hong Kong within hours
articles.summary:
Hong Kong’s weather forecaster has warned that downpours are approaching the city, saying they are expected to become more frequent throughout the day.
A spokesman for the Hong Kong Observatory said at 8.20am on Tuesday that an area of thundery showers over western Guangdong was gradually moving eastwards.
“Showers are expected to be heavier in some areas, with squally thunderstorms affecting parts of the territory in the next couple of hours,” the spokesman said.
Hong Kong has experienced...

articles.title: Chinese teenager lies to parents about holiday to secretly help flood victims in Guangxi
articles.summary:
A teenager from eastern China told his parents he was going on holiday, but secretly travelled with a friend to a flood-stricken region to join relief efforts.
Earlier this month, 18-year-old Shanghai student Wu Yulun had just completed the gaokao, China’s national college entrance examination, and was planning a graduation trip.
But after seeing an online appeal from a volunteer group in the southwestern Guangxi region, he abandoned those plans and headed for the disaster zone instead.
Days of....

articles.title: Fifa effect: Hong Kong developers bet on sport fever to keep shoppers spending
articles.summary:
From attracting tourist spending to live streaming world sports events like the Fifa World Cup, Hong Kong developers have tried to claw their way through a challenging retail market and found a rare boost to shopping malls.
As crowds converged on neighbourhood malls to watch live broadcasts of World Cup matches, restaurants, sportswear retailers and consumer electronics stores were among the biggest beneficiaries.
Sino Group, which spent more than HK$8 million (US$1 million) broadcasting all 104...

articles.title: Hong Kong’s first-time lawmakers ask fewer questions but attend Legco more often
articles.summary:
Hong Kong’s first-time lawmakers have raised fewer questions than their veteran counterparts but recorded a higher attendance rate, a South China Morning Post check has found.
Alan Chan Chung-yee, who is also the chief operating officer of Miramar Group, and athlete turned legislator Vivian Kong Man-wai were among those who submitted the fewest questions to the government.
Political observers attributed the trend to newcomers seeking to balance respect for the executive-led system with their...

articles.title: Chinese tourists blamed for overtourism in Japan’s Kyoto despite huge fall in arrivals
articles.summary:
Chinese tourists are being unfairly blamed for Kyoto’s overtourism crisis, according to a new report, even as data shows mainland visitor numbers have fallen sharply amid worsening ties between China and Japan.
The report released earlier this month by the Kyoto City Tourism Association’s destination management organisation said many local residents assumed crowded public transport, congestion at popular sights and rising hotel prices were being driven by mainland Chinese tour groups.

articles.title: Why China must end the lucrative marriage between property and banks
articles.summary:
Capital seeks sanctuary. In China’s property sector, however, that quest for safety had inadvertently erected a cage. The recent government takeover of Wuhan-based private lender Zhongbang Bank, expected to see a dilution of its private capital amid serious credit risks, is a calculated regulatory action. It echoes a larger and darker precedent.
A few years ago, another commercial lender, Shengjing Bank, became caught up in the Evergrande debt crisis. There was speculation property developer...
